text: This twenty-five-year-old female patient presents with symptomatic biliary dyskinesia for a single incision cholecystectomy.
The surgeon accesses the abdomen through a two-centimeter incision at the umbilicus, using a tripod setup to stabilize the instruments.
The surgeon uses a conventional five millimeter grasper to retract the gallbladder superiorly, providing better exposure for the cholecystectomy procedure.
A two-row silk suture on a straight needle is passed through the infundibulum of the gallbladder and exits laterally in the epigastrium, securing the gallbladder in place.
The surgeon manipulates the neck of the gallbladder using a suture technique, providing adequate exposure for further dissection.
The surgeon uses a conventional straight hook dissector to incise the peritoneum overlying the neck of the gallbladder, exposing the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon uses a conventional straight hook dissector to incise the posterior peritoneal attachments of the gallbladder, further exposing the surgical field.
The surgeon visualizes the cystic duct and the adjacent cystic duct node, with clear exposure facilitated by previous retraction and dissection steps.
The surgeon carefully dissects the cystic artery free from surrounding tissues, ensuring clear visualization of the artery within the surgical field.
The surgeon places conventional 5mm locking hemo clips across the cystic duct and then divides it, completing the separation of the duct from the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to dissect the remaining attachments between the gallbladder and the liver bed in a retrograde fashion, following the division of the cystic artery.
Medial and lateral retraction of the silk suture provides clear exposure of the surgical field, facilitating the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver bed.
The surgeon divides the remaining attachments between the gallbladder and liver bed, successfully freeing the gallbladder from the liver bed.
Following irrigation, the surgeon grasps the gallbladder and withdraws it through the umbilical incision, completing its removal from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon closes the fascial defect at the umbilicus using interrupted zero PDS sutures, followed by closure of the skin with fine absorbable sutures.
